text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
Yesterday we saw that we, as new creation believers, carry the table of showbread not as an external object but as a living reality formed into our very lives. We carry the incorruptible nature of the new creation the sittim wood that holds in the desert. We carry the gold of transformation, being covered from inside out with the character of Christ. And we carry the crown of life, the royal identity that holds us in place through the entire wilderness journey. Today we carry the study further and look at three more things on the table:
the unleavened bread, the twelve equal loaves, and the frankincense. These are among the most searching truths we will encounter. They ask us not just what we carry but who we are while we carry it.

The showbread was unleavened; made without yeast, baked quickly so that not even the natural leaven in the air could enter the dough. In Scripture, leaven is the picture of sin, pride, false teaching, and moral corruption that spreads silently from the inside out. It is invisible at first. You cannot see it working.
But given time, it fills the whole lump. The Feast of Unleavened Bread in Exodus 12 required every household to search for and remove every trace of leaven before the feast began. Not just obvious leaven. Every hidden piece, in every corner, under every shelf.

Paul applies this directly to us in 1 Corinthians 5:7-8: “Cleanse out the old leaven that you may be a new lump, as you really are unleavened. For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed. Let us therefore celebrate the feast, not with the old leaven, the leaven of malice and evil, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.” Paul says two things in the same breath that must be held together. He says “as you really are unleavened” meaning the new creation has already been declared unleavened in Christ, cleansed by His sacrifice. And then he says “cleanse out the old leaven” meaning we must live out what we already are. The position is settled. The practice must catch up with the position.

What is leaven in our lives today? Leaven is the small compromise we allow because it seems harmless. Leaven is the bitterness we keep in one corner of the heart while appearing fine on the outside. Leaven is the pride that rises quietly, the impure thought we entertain rather than cast out, the false teaching we tolerate because it is popular, the half-truth we speak because the full truth would make us lose something. Leaven never stays small. It spreads.
One compromise leads to the next. One tolerated sin softens the conscience toward the next. The table of showbread had to be made without leaven because it stood before the face of Yahweh. We stand before the face of Yahweh. The leaven must go. This is not optional holiness. It is the character of those who carry the table.

The twelve loaves on the table were not decoration. They were substantial bread six pounds of fine flour each, placed in perfect order before the face of God, representing all of His people, always. Every Sabbath they were replaced with fresh loaves. The table was never without this bread. It was the Lechem Tamid the continual bread, the bread that never ran out and never ran stale.

We live in a world that is starving. But it is not starving for lack of words.
It is drowning in words; in opinions, in philosophies, in self-help, in social media voices, in false prophets, in empty religious noise that sounds like bread but has no flour in it. 2 Timothy 4:3-4 says: “The time is coming when people will not endure sound teaching, but having itching ears they will accumulate for themselves teachers to suit their own passions, and will turn away from listening to the truth and wander off into myths.” That time is not coming. It is here. The world is eating from tables that have no real bread on them.
And Yahweh has placed His people in the middle of this world carrying the table of the continual, genuine, never-stale, never-false truth of His Word.

This is what the twelve equal loaves mean for us. We carry the Word of God; all of it, not selected parts, not the comfortable verses without the hard ones, not twelve loaves with two removed because they are unpopular. All twelve. In the right order. Before the face of God. We carry the truth when the world is full of delusion. We carry sound doctrine when itching ears want stories.
We carry the bread that truly nourishes into rooms where people have been eating spiritual junk food their entire lives. The table was never empty for a single Sabbath through forty years. The congregation of the new covenant must not allow the table of the Word to go empty. There is a hungry world that does not yet know what it is hungry for, and we have been given the bread they need.

On top of the twelve loaves sat golden cups filled with pure frankincense.
The bread was given to be eaten. The frankincense was given to ascend.
When the Sabbath came and the old bread was removed, the frankincense was taken and burned before Yahweh as a memorial; a rising fragrance, a sweet-smelling reminder of the covenant and the people. The bread fed the priests. The incense fed the heart of God.

Romans 12:1 says: “I appeal to you therefore, brothers, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God, which is your spiritual worship.” This is the frankincense on the table. The bread we carry; the truth, the Word, the new creation nature is given for others to eat.
But the frankincense is what rises from our lives toward God. It is the daily offering of a life laid down in obedience and worship. It is the prayer that goes up before we speak. It is the quiet act of service no one sees. It is the obedience that costs something. It is the love shown to a difficult person because we belong to the one who loved His enemies. All of this is frankincense rising from the table we carry; and it reaches the Father.

Ephesians 5:2 says Christ gave Himself as “a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God.” His whole life was frankincense. And now, as we are being transformed into His image, our lives are meant to carry the same fragrance. Not the smell of the world. Not the smell of compromise or bitterness or ambition.
The fragrance of a life given over to God, burning quietly and continuously before His face as a memorial. When we live this way; carrying the incorruptible nature, being covered in the gold of transformation, crowned with the life that does not fade, unleavened in our character, full of the bread of truth, and rising before God as a fragrant offering; we are carrying the table. The whole table. All of it. On our shoulders. Through the wilderness.
Until we reach the place where we no longer need to carry it, because we will sit at it forever.

The table was the same height as the Ark. The bread and the presence stood level with each other in the Holy Place. This is the final word on what we carry. The truth we carry and the presence we carry are not two separate things at different levels. They are the same height. You cannot separate the Word of God from the presence of God. You cannot carry the bread without also carrying the Ark. The new creation Kohathite carries both on their shoulders; the presence and the provision, the glory and the bread; at the same level, in the same life, through the same wilderness.

title: Prayer
text:
Search us, O Yahweh, and know our hearts; try us and know our anxious thoughts; and see if there be any wicked way in us, and lead us in the way everlasting (Psalm 139:23-24). You have called us to carry the table and You know what is still in us that should not be there. Search out the leaven; the hidden bitterness, the quiet pride, the compromises we have made peace with. Do not let us carry Your truth with an unleavened mouth but a leavened heart. Make us what we carry. May the wood in us hold in the desert. May the gold in us cover every hidden surface. May the crown in us keep us from falling away. May the bread in us feed those who are starving for truth in a world of noise. And may our lives rise before You as a fragrant offering; not occasionally, not only in the good seasons, but daily, continuously, like the frankincense that rose from the table every Sabbath without fail. We are Your Kohathites.
We carry what You have placed on our shoulders. Help us carry it well.
In Christ’s name, amen.
